questions.question_text: DC's rent stabilization program under the Rental Housing Act of 1985 primarily applies to buildings built before which date?
distractors: January 1, 1985
December 31, 1990
January 1, 1970
questions.explanation: DC's rent control primarily covers apartment buildings built before December 31, 1975. Buildings constructed after this date are generally exempt from rent stabilization.

questions.question_text: A building was constructed in 1980 in DC. Is it subject to rent control?
questions.correct_answer: No, buildings built after 1975 are generally exempt
distractors: Yes, all rental buildings in DC are covered
Yes, if it has more than 4 units
Only if the owner elects to register
questions.explanation: Buildings constructed after December 31, 1975, are generally exempt from DC's rent stabilization program. The law targets older housing stock where tenant protections are most needed.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is exempt from DC rent control?
distractors: Pre-1976 apartment buildings
Units occupied by elderly tenants
Buildings registered with RAD
questions.explanation: Exemptions from DC rent control include single-family homes, condominiums, government-subsidized housing, certain small landlords (4 or fewer units), and buildings built after December 31, 1975.

questions.question_text: All rental units in DC must be registered with which division?
distractors: The Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection
The Office of the Tenant Advocate (OTA)
The DC Housing Authority (DCHA)
questions.explanation: All rental units in DC, whether or not they are subject to rent control, must be registered with the Rental Accommodations Division (RAD) of the Department of Housing and Community Development.

questions.question_text: A DC landlord owns a building with 3 rental units built in 1960. Can they claim the small landlord exemption from rent control?
questions.correct_answer: Possibly, if they meet the 4-or-fewer-units conditions
distractors: No, all pre-1976 buildings are subject to rent control
Yes, any building under 10 units is exempt
Only if none of the tenants are elderly
questions.explanation: DC provides a potential exemption for small landlords with 4 or fewer rental units, subject to certain conditions. The landlord would need to meet all requirements to qualify for this exemption.

questions.question_text: What formula determines the maximum annual rent increase for most DC rent-controlled units?
questions.correct_answer: CPI-W plus 2%, or 10%, whichever is less
distractors: A flat 5% per year
CPI-W only, with no additional percentage
10% per year with no cap
questions.explanation: For standard (non-elderly/disabled) tenants, DC allows annual rent increases of the CPI-W plus 2%, capped at a maximum of 10%. This formula balances landlord costs with tenant affordability.

questions.question_text: How does the rent increase cap differ for elderly or disabled tenants in DC?
questions.correct_answer: They receive a lower cap based on CPI-W only
distractors: They have the same cap as all other tenants
They receive a complete freeze on rent increases
They can only have increases every 3 years
questions.explanation: Elderly and disabled tenants receive a lower maximum increase based on CPI-W alone (without the additional 2%). For 2026, this means a 2.1% cap versus 4.1% for other tenants.

questions.question_text: When a rent-controlled unit becomes vacant in DC, how is the allowable rent increase determined?
questions.correct_answer: 10% if tenant was there 10 years or less; 20% if longer
distractors: A flat 20% increase regardless of prior tenancy
No increase is permitted on vacancy in DC
Unlimited increase to current market rent level
questions.explanation: DC's vacancy increase depends on the prior tenant's length of occupancy: 10% if they lived there 10 years or less, 20% if more than 10 years. Banked annual increases may also apply.

questions.question_text: A DC landlord wants a rent increase larger than the CPI formula allows. What can they do?
questions.correct_answer: File a hardship petition with RAD
distractors: Simply raise the rent and notify the tenant
Get a court order from DC Superior Court
Apply to DLCP for a special exemption
questions.explanation: Landlords can file hardship petitions with RAD to request larger increases if operating costs require it. They can also pursue voluntary agreements with 70%+ of tenants for above-standard increases.

questions.question_text: A landlord and 75% of tenants in a DC building agree to a rent increase above the standard cap. Under DC law, this is called a:
distractors: Hardship exception
Rent stabilization waiver
Market adjustment agreement
questions.explanation: A voluntary agreement allows a landlord and 70%+ of tenants to agree to increases beyond the standard CPI-based cap. This requires the consent of a supermajority of tenants in the building.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum security deposit a DC landlord can charge?
distractors: 2 months' rent
First and last month's rent
No statutory limit in DC
questions.explanation: DC limits security deposits to 1 month's rent. The deposit must be held in an interest-bearing account at a DC financial institution, and the landlord must provide a written receipt.

questions.question_text: How must a DC landlord hold a tenant's security deposit?
questions.correct_answer: In an interest-bearing escrow account in DC
distractors: In any personal bank account
In a non-interest bearing trust account
In a separate account only if over $1,000
questions.explanation: DC requires security deposits to be held in an interest-bearing escrow account at a DC financial institution. The landlord must provide the tenant with the name and address of the depository.

questions.question_text: A DC tenant moves out. Within how many days must the landlord return the security deposit or provide an itemized deduction statement?
distractors: 30 days
60 days
21 days
questions.explanation: DC landlords have 45 days after move-out to either return the security deposit plus interest or notify the tenant of intent to withhold with an itemized statement of deductions.

questions.question_text: A DC landlord fails to return a security deposit in bad faith. What penalty can the court impose?
distractors: Double damages
A $500 fine
Loss of the rental license
questions.explanation: If a DC landlord fails to return a security deposit in bad faith, they can be liable for treble (triple) the amount owed. This penalty incentivizes landlords to comply with the return requirements.

questions.question_text: Under DC law, can a landlord change the locks to evict a tenant who has not paid rent?
distractors: Yes, after giving 5 days' written notice
Yes, if the tenant is more than 30 days late
Only if the landlord also files a court notice
questions.explanation: DC strictly prohibits self-help evictions. Landlords cannot change locks, remove belongings, or cut utilities to force a tenant out. All evictions must go through DC Superior Court, Landlord-Tenant Branch.
